text_clean: It might sound insane to rate this site as 2 star consider is a world heritage site but this was one of the most deflating experiences of my trip. We got the circuito 1 ticket which basically keeps you out of the site and you get is a view. AND NOTICE ONCE UP THERE YOU CANNOT BUY A SECOND TICKET . When we realized this we tried to get another ticket but we were told we needed to go down to the town and buy another one and then go back up again. Which means a couple of hours by bus and your train tickets might be lost as they also have set times. To be honest they should not sell this ticket! thank God they sun came out and we had a site other wise we would have spend $300 to see a cloud! I really dislike how artificial and turistic this attraction is and how circuito 1 feels more like a scam

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Vista la importancia del hallazgo, Walter Alva impulsó la construcción del Museo Tumbas Reales de Sipan, que fue inaugurado en el 2002. Está ubicado en Lambayeque, y su edificación se inspiró en las antiguas pirámides truncadas de la prehispánica Civilización Moche (siglo I a VII d. C.). El museo custodia más de dos mil piezas de oro. Sin lugar a dudas, el principal atractivo es la tumba del Señor de Sipán, con sus acompañantes y sus respectivos ajuares funerarios. La estructura, en un área techada de 3.156,45 m², tiene tres pisos. El acceso es a través de una rampa de 74.21 metros de largo, tal y como se accedía a los antiguos templos moches. Dicha pendiente conduce al tercer piso. La visita se realiza de arriba hacia abajo reviviendo la experiencia del descubridor del Señor de Sipan. Desde el segundo piso, se observa la réplica exacta de la cámara funeraria del antiguo dignatario moche llamado el Señor de Sipan y saber cómo fue su cultura, sus logros tecnológicos y su pensamiento sobre la muerte, entre otros aspectos. El centro del museo contiene la Sala Real Mochica donde se encuentra el Señor de Sipan con su ropa de guerrero y pectoral de oro y otras joyas del mismo material; la cámara funeraria del antiguo gobernante, junto a ocho esqueletos de sus acompañantes; otros dignatarios descubiertos, como el sacerdote y el Viejo Señor de Sipán, de la misma investidura que el gobernador moche, todos con sus respectivas vestimentas originales. En esta Sala Real Mochica, hay 35 maniquíes que representan su entorno político, diez de los cuales han sido articulados, por lo que están en movimiento durante las visitas. Esta reconstrucción exacta de lo que fue toda una magnificiencia de la corte real del guerrero moche representa fielmente lo que fue un séquito Mochica. También se pueden ver los ornamentos recuperados del sepulcro, como es el caso del protector coxal de oro, entre otros importantes objetos. Se incluye un mensaje de lo que dejó el saqueo y la importancia de la protección de la herencia cultural del Perú. Hay parqueo de autos, con seguridad, ambiente amplio. Se recomienda visitar el museo para conocer y aprender más de nuestra historia, costumbre y procedencia de nuestros pasados.

text_clean: As far as most historic centers go in Latin American metropolisis, this is definitely one that has been taken care and preserved by the government and it's citizenry. It's historical buildings and facades showcase the architecture that was prevalent during the colonial era and it's promenades, plazas, government palaces and Catholic places of worship are grand buildings worthy of preservation and conservation efforts for generations to come. It's beautiful but like all great modern cosmopolitan cities, Limas historic center also has pickpockets and people of ill-will looking out for bedazzled tourists sporting their wealth through their jewelry, flimsy backpacks, and expensive camera equipment. This place is worth the visit but just be extremely aware of your surroundings at all times and wary of "friendly" strangers who approach you attempting to make casual conversation. In particular, stay away from the young, urban men selling "Tattoo" services, which after a while becomes almost amusing, as if it was the only clandestine service in need of a large throng of roaming salesmen throughout the plazas and public walkways of this grand historical center. Please visit and enjoy during the day but be extremely careful in the late afternoon hours and postpone coming at night, unless accompanied by tour guides or citizens of Lima aware of what's going on. Many of these warnings are common sense, applicable to all major Latin American historical centers but people lower their guards when they become enchanted taking pictures or gazing upon the beautiful buildings. To it's credit, the government is aware of it's issue and has deployed a good show of force with it's tourism police officers who walk throughout the center wiling to offer assistance. Great place to take pictures, but never let your guard down.

text_clean: Manchu Picchu is a beautiful destination and a place that everyone should see one in their lives. Got lucky. Weather was perfect … in March. The train ride from Cusco was also pretty. text_clean: Es un lugar para sacarse fotos. No tiene un buen trabajo museografico. Los textos del guión del convento son muy pobres, 4 líneas, cuando las hay. Sin guía debería uno poder aunque sea tener una idea de cómo era la vida cotidiana de las monjas, somera, saber que tenían servidumbre, cómo fue cambiando a lo largo del tiempo, un poco de historia social. Los objetos no son suficientes como para entenderlo todo. Creo que ese es su problema, descansan en la arquitectura que es preciosa y ya. La entrada es muy cara, 40 soles, no incluye visita guiada, para tener una debes pagar 20 soles más. La relación precio/servicio no es buena. Es una pena porque el lugar es hermoso, pero no te llevas nada de información, solo fotos bonitas.

text_clean: A spectacular canyon, and in my view even more spectacular historical site. The terraces built by the pre-inca civilitation that stretch through the valley are stunning. I cannot begin to imagine the amount of labour and sheer effort that went into building these. There are some excellent opportunities to take short hikes to scenic viewpoints, and inbetween you get around easily by bus or car, so it's very well suited for the not so outdoor-minded among us, which includes me. There is also an active volcanoe that erupts from time to time, making for a spectacular view.

Audio guides would be the perfect solution.

text_clean: Arequipa is an incredible experience overall and surprisingly affordable! Known as the White City, Arequipa boasts charming architecture and a vibrant culture that makes every visit special. One of the highlights of my trip was visiting the breathtaking Colca Canyon. The landscapes of Colca Canyon are absolutely unbelievable. The vast, rugged beauty of the canyon leaves you in awe, and it's a fantastic place to connect with nature. The tranquility and peacefulness you find there are unmatched. It's a perfect escape from the hustle and bustle of daily life. Walking along the canyon, you can truly appreciate the serene environment and the stunning views that stretch as far as the eye can see. Watching the majestic condors glide through the air is an experience in itself. Arequipa adds to the experience with its unique blend of historical sites and modern amenities. The combination of the city's unique charm and the breathtaking beauty of Colca Canyon makes for a memorable visit. Whether you're hiking the trails, soaking in the hot springs, or simply taking in the views, it's a place that invites you to relax and unwind. Overall, visiting Colca Canyon in Arequipa is an unforgettable experience that I highly recommend. The natural beauty, peaceful atmosphere, and affordability make it a must-see destination for anyone looking to explore the wonders of Peru.

text_clean: I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone

text_clean: Ayer fui al circuito mágico del agua. Una pésima idea, fui con un adulto mayor y niños, no hay asientos disponibles y en una calor extrema, no hay donde taparse o asombrarse del del sol cuando quisimos sentarnos en el en la zonas verdes nos dijeron que estaba prohibido y tuvimos que estar parados cerca de las tres horas que estuvimos con el adulto mayor y no pudimos sentarnos a ningún lugar ya que todo estaba repleto; pero tengo que hacer una observación. Hay muchos juegos para niños que están posicionados en muchas áreas verdes,mientras a la gente que paga una entrada, no se nos permitía ni siquiera sentarnos dentro de estas zonas ya que no hay asientos suficiente, me parece muy pésima idea es un sitio donde uno quiere ir a a observar las aguas, las piletas, etc. pero lo han convertido en un sitio donde se está monetizando y muchas personas que venimos de fuera quedamos decepcionados , Prefiero ir a otros lugares donde te traten mejor. No les sugiero que vayan, tengan otras opciones para poder salir un fin de semana a divertirse acá en Lima.

text_clean: What a waste of time! You go there and get tickets without a wait. The guide at the entrance who ended up being our guide during the tour told us the tour is mandatory and we have to tip her at the end. She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.

Tardas unos 20 min en subir, es pesado y da mucho vértigo pero la experiencia lo vale.
label: positivo

text_clean: Tuvimos la oportunidad de realizar la ruta del Salkantay y llegar hasta este maravilloso lugar. Machu Picchu te entrega una sensación mágica al estar en presencia de ello, es un lugar que a través de sus construcciones y la historia detrás de cada rincón que ves por sus senderos te susurran lo impresionante que puede ser el ser humano. Es un lugar muy bien cuidado pero que se ve un poco opacado por la explotación turística del lugar mismo. la entrada es excesivamente costosa para el extranjero y te cobran hasta para ingresar al baño y por situación de pandemia te limitan los senderos y las vistas, pero, por el mismo precio.

text_clean: Hermoso lugar, lleno de una gran y especial energía. Nosotros contratamos una agencia, lo que facilitó todo el viaje. Nos pasaron a buscar en bus al hotel, nos entregaron nuestros boletos del tren y del bus para subir a Machu Picchu. Incluso pudimos dejar las mochilas con un encargado que las llevó a nuestro hotel de Aguas Calientes. Así subimos livianos, solo con lo necesario. Tuvimos una guía, Eli de agencia Pacifico, que nos dio una charla maravillosa de cómo vivieron en esa linda ciudad. Y a mediodía nos dejó libres para que recorramos a nuestras anchas. A las 2 almorzamos y todo ok. Lo único es la espera para poder bajar. La fila para tomar los buses llegaba hasta la curva del camino, pero es solo paciencia. Es un viaje 100% recomendado.

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.
label: neutro

Siempre es mejor tener precaución con sus cosas cuando se está por el centro ya que es un lugar turístico. text_clean: Totally worth the hype. Amazing to see in person. Tips we wished we’d known: Get there at 6 for the misty views that then open up into clear sights. Stay for a few hours at one of the viewpoints because it’s a one-way, no re-entry system. Go for circuit 2 for the best views. Get to the bus stop early as the queues start an hour before. The weather changes super fast so pack rain gear. You can book your Machu Picchu ticket MUCH cheaper on the website rather than through a tour. Either bring plenty of money for food as it’s very expensive or bring pack-ups. Go to the cascades for a swim while in aguas calientes. There are 1000 tickets released daily for walk-ins, it opens at 6am but people were queuing before 5am. Remember to bring your passports for both the train and the entrance—they check multiple times (you can even get a passport stamp!). The bus to get there is 12 USD per person each way, or it is a steep uphill climb which takes about 1hr 40min. We got the bus up and walked down.

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. text_clean: Ancient sacred city in the clouds! The 600 hundred year old architecture and stone work is amazing! Walk on one of the ancient Inca trails and you will come across the famous Inca Bridge. To get the best experience, I recommend a tour guide from a reputable company to appreciate the knowledge and history of Machu Picchu. Be prepared to wait in long cues.You are not alone on the mountain. There are thousands of tourists pounding the walk ways. And sometimes it is a slow walk as the narrow paths are blocked by people taking selfies or that iconic photo. There are no toilets or bathrooms on the site. These are at the gate entrance only. Time is also limited, you can go either to the morning or afternoon session. Feel safe, as there are security discreetly watching. Some areas are restricted with no touch or no entry signs, due to tourists having silly accidents, and also others causing damage to structures. You will see archaeologists at work and maintenance people too. The mountain is accessible to people who have some mobility. Even people with walking sticks and elderly were able to experience the mountain. Remember to take your passport to get the famous Machu Picchu stamp. I would love to go to Machu Picchu again one day.

text_clean: Unbelievable in person. Pictures do not do it justice. Here’s some helpful tips as an American tourist on their first trip to Macchu Picchu: 1 Buy your tickets well in advance. There is a huge line at the tourist office in Aguas Calientes for last minute ticket purchases. It’s chaotic, skip the headache and plan ahead. We were out in Aguas Calientes for two nights and both nights the line / crowd was huge. Do your research on the best time to go. We went early October (last week) - the temperature was perfect. Crispy in the early morning then warmed up later on. Sun is strong, wear sunscreen and bug spray. 2 If you can, and are up for the challenge, do the hiking trail up. Not the Inca Trail. There’s a walking path that’s about ~1+ hour (longer if you take breaks). It’s 100% worth the effort for the views. It’s like the stair-master on hard. There are buses that run very frequently up but they’re not for the faint of heart or for impatient people who hate waiting in long lines. The buses fly up the sides of the mountain (no thank you). 3 Get an early ticket. It gets crowded. We had a 9am and it was great. It was crowded when we were leaving about 2 hours later. 4 There’s a snack counter and a formal restaurant. The snack counter is 10/10 with beer, cocktails, desserts, and snacks (chicken empanada is A1). Grab something before you go in as you can’t eat when you’re in there. 5 You cannot get in ahead of your ticket time. They’re strict about this. 6 BRING YOUR PASSPORT 7 Get a guide. There’s no informational signs that describe what you’re looking at. If you don’t have someone describing things you’re just there for the views (even though we left with more questions than answers) 8 Bring soles for the bathroom. 2 soles to enter. 9 Respect the beauty of the site

text_clean: Machu Picchu - 10 things that may help to make your trip more pleasurable updated: 25 September 2022 1. Book entry tickets well in advance - there is a daily quota of 2,000 people only 2. Decide on pace of travel - day trip from Cusco is doable but it’s still at least a 12-hour ordeal door to door. To have a less stressful visit if time allows, stay in fun Aguas Calientes to chill for an evening or 2 before embarking on your choice of transportation is a pretty good idea. 3. Decide on how you want to get there - a). Hiking all the way (28 miles for 4d3n) b). Train all the way (Inkarail / Perurail for 1.5 hrs) c). Train and Hike combo (train then hike the last 8 miles) d). Bus 4. Decide on when you want to get there - a). For Sunrise, be prepared to stay a night in Machu Picchu (national park opens at 6am) b). For Sunset, time yourself to find a nice seat facing west near the sundial. Remember you have 3 hours inside the park and you will need to get yourself out after sunset. 5. Local tour guide is essential - the last chance to find them if you do not already have one will be in the bus queue from Machu Picchu train station or outside the national park. Apart from facts and myths you can read up prior to the visit, the guide will be able to point out interesting facts such as: best spots to take photographs / the stones that look like a llama. 6. Separate bookings / time restricted visits - a). Visit to the sundial is included in the tickets for national park entry but it’s only opened from 7-10am daily b). Wayna Picchu (Young Mountain) , Hucchu Picchu (Baby Mountain) & Gran Caverna: this requires booking separately. There are 2 groups of 200 pax daily capacity. Round trip time takes around 2 hrs / 45 min / 4 hrs respectively. 7. Mosquito repellent: this is absolutely essential if you do not want to remember the breath taking view of Machu Picchu by the itchy mozzie scars! These bugs are tiny black things, and they draw blood in nano seconds! Army grade DEET is recommended especially if you are a sunrise / sunset romantic, or if you intend to do any form of hiking. The bites may only appear up to 36 hours later! 8. Climate check: a). check whether but be prepared to encounter all 4 seasons on your visit b). check political actions - as protests can shut down all roads and transportations! 9. Be prepared to queue: no pain no gain! 10. Altitude: if you are like me whose normal inhabitant is at sea level, climatise yourself gently. Cusco is at 3400m and although Michu Picchu is at a lower altitude of 2400m, strolling can still be taxing at times! Hope you have a wonderful trip like I did!

text_clean: Huaca Pucllana came recommended from a tour guide. He said the restaurant next to it was amazing. He’s 2 for 2!! So Huaca Pucllana - you get queued up in a group, but the people never stop coming so you don’t wait long. They do English groups and Spanish groups. The guides are great , funny knowledgeable , and good at herding cats. If you’re reading this before you go / bring stuff to protect yourself from the sun. A hat, sunscreen, etc and bring water. There no shade and it’s hot. The day was cool in Lima but kinda hot here despite being right in town. It’s also dusty so don’t wear your finest. Ok, with that out of the way… awesome site and incredible history - you can walk through a pre Incan pyramid in the middle of the city !!! It’s all made from adobe - Sun dried clay - so it’s dusty. They do a great job keeping it interesting and as you explore you learn so much and see new things. I Wondered if it was going to be repetitive- but it was great. You also get a great view of the city - I didn’t think it was that high up but it totally is. Worth it for the views alone.

text_clean: Climbing Machu Picchu Mountain on My 49th Birthday Decided to take on the Machu Picchu peak hike to mark my 49th birthday—honestly, I thought I’d only go halfway since I wasn’t fully prepared and didn’t carry enough water. But once we started the climb, especially up those endless stone stairs, things got real. It was tough, and more than once I felt like giving up. You also have to reach certain control points at scheduled times, or you risk being turned back—so there’s a bit of pressure to keep moving. Thankfully, the weather was in our favor. Even with just a little water, I managed to push through. There are roughly 1,600 uneven stone steps. The first half doesn’t offer many views and feels like a slog. But once you reach the steeper parts, you’re rewarded with stunning vistas of ancient Inca villages and lush valleys. And when you finally reach the summit—wow. A full 360° panoramic view, clear skies, and the raw beauty of the Andes makes every aching muscle worth it. My advice? Go slow. Breathe. Take your time. The last 400 steps will test you—they’re steep, and the altitude hits hard. But don’t give up. Just keep moving at your pace. The top is unforgettable Carry enough water n energy bars to celebrate at the top. Going up it took 2 hrs coming down and is also not easy took toll on knees 1 hrs total round trip 3 hrs

text_clean: Ha mejorado mucho este hermoso parque, mucha seguridad y siempre el agua es magica. Muy economico entrar (4 soles o 1 dolar). Hay buenas rutas para llegar aqui, tanto en el metropolitano como servicio de taxi y uber; ademas, el Estadio principal de la ciudad es un buen referente. Recomiendo llegar comenzando la noche y en semana, para que no lo coja la tarde despues. Los baños, relativamente aseados. Considero que es uno de los parques mejor cuidados de la ciudad (nada que ver con el pesimo parque de las leyendas). Como en esta epoca del año, ventea mucho y hace frio, ir bien abrigado. Apoyar a los que trabajan allí, en especial los fotografos. Los comestibles, no hay mucha oferta pero por favor, no generar basuras ni tirarlas, es triste ver esa indisciplina de muchos peruanos que no ayudan a cuidar sus propios parques. Agrego una foto, con el mapa del lugar.

text_clean: Peru Rail and Inca Rail are the two train operators. I went with Inca Rail’s bimodal service based on trip times. I took The Voyager light option. Overall, it was a good experience. I took a bus from Cusco to Ollantaytambo, then a train to Aguas Calientes. I went the day before to avoid any potential delays. You can book everything as a package through either company, but I found it cheaper and more flexible to book tickets separately. You can either walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu or take the bus ($12 USD one way). The buses are run by Consettur, and tickets can be bought online or at the ticket office. The ride takes about 25 minutes, and I recommend getting in line at least an hour before your entrance time. There are signs for different lines based on entry times. Make sure you have your ticket and passport before getting in line. While waiting, guides will offer their services. I was quoted $25 USD per person for a group of four or $100 USD for a private tour. I got in line around 7 am and reached the entrance around 8. Once you arrive, there’s a bathroom that costs 2 soles. There are no official bathrooms inside. There’s also a spot to get food and store luggage or a bag if needed. I visited at the end of January and was worried about the weather, so I booked two different routes on two separate days. I did the 2A classic route and 1A Machu Picchu Mountain. I had an 8 am entry time both days and thankfully had great weather. It seemed to get cloudier and rainier in the afternoon The 2A route was good. I went without a guide, but I heard others say they really enjoyed having one. It seemed like about half the visitors had guides, half didn’t. It took me about two hours to walk around, taking my time. The 2A classic route is popular for a reason, but I didn’t see any signs differentiating it from 2B, just a general sign for route 2. The highlight for me was hiking Machu Picchu Mountain. It wasn’t crowded at all. I saw maybe 10 people during the hike. The views were stunning, and while the hike is challenging, it’s absolutely worth it. You’re walking up stone steps for pretty much the entire way, so wearing shoes or hiking boots with good traction is a must. The trail is well-maintained. There are great viewpoints along the hike, so even if you can’t make it all the way up, you’ll still get good views. text_clean: Este Circuito es uno de los más hermosos de toda Latinoamérica. Para mí la fuente principal y mas hermosa e impactante es la más grande de forma corcular de esta lindo parque de la reserva, en esta oportunidad me tocó asistir un día sábado y es un día que por más que sea fin de semana no lo recomiendo para asistir. Es un día súper repleto de personas y para entrar hay dos entradas para pagar en taquilla, el precio es de 4 soles por personas y las personas con discapacidad pueden ingresar pagando el 50% de la entrada, por ser un día súper difícil de asistir y de noche, para el ingreso se hacen unas colas gigantes, en donde hay usureros que se ponen en la entrada del parque a venderte los puestos para acercarte más en la entrada. Es una completa locura no me gustó asistir este día. Tuve que correr para la otra entrada antes de que se volviera a poner mas larga. Yo ya he ido en el transcurso de la semana y no es tan desastroso como los fines de semana. Lo único que deberían hacer es aprovechar las taquillas vacías para colocar cajeras en los días que son más concurridos por personas residenciales y turistas. Es la única cosa negativa. De lo contrario. Hay vigilancia. Protección. Cuidado. Es limpio (no entre a los baños pero si hay baños) es un lugar familia, y si, si te puedes bañar en las fuentes dinámicas para adultos y niños, además hay un tren en el parque en donde puedes recorrerlo sin ningún problema, hay lugares autorizados para comer. Y además castillos inflables para los más pequeños. Hay que ir como porque las fuentes se prestan para mojarte en las fotografías. Dentro del parque también hay fotógrafos que imprimen las fotografías en el momento. Lo recomiendo.
